chunks.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak. Can you hear me? Yes. My name's William McGumsey. I'm a former policy advisor, having worked for several government departments on a range of projects over 15 years, and I now work in independent media. My submission focuses on the immigration risks posed by the free trade agreement.

However, rather than opposing the agreement outright, as many who share my concerns around immigration do, I think those risks can be addressed by changes to general immigration settings. Outside of the risks I identify, I support the FTA because it diversifies our export markets, reduces our dependence on China, and because there is significant upside potential for trade and economic benefits as the Indian economy grows.

The debate over the FTA comes in a context when most of our traditional allies are moving to tighten their immigration settings. The reason for this tightening is that Europe, the US, the UK, and Australia have in recent years all experienced growing immigration-related problems such as increased frequency of terror attacks, rising migrant crime, threats to social cohesion, national identity, and trust in institutions.

In December 2025, the US released an updated national security strategy with border security at its center. The strategy pointed out the problems mass migration is causing in Europe, weakened social cohesion, political fragmentation, reduced state legitimacy, and strain on welfare and public services.

The strategy also said that NATO allies may, over time, become majority non-European, which might change their political alignment and reliability as US partners. Here in New Zealand, our own Security Intelligence Service, in its August 2025 Security Threat Environment report, identified the risk of foreign political interference via diaspora populations as a key problem facing New Zealand.

This is of particular relevance to the debate around the FTA, as India's Prime Minister Modi is on the record about his desire to use the Indian diaspora as a soft power tool to influence Western governments. Here, I'd like to note the link between diaspora lobbying and hate speech laws. Often, diaspora populations, through their ethnic lobbying organizations, are some of the biggest proponents of hate speech laws. And while genuine hate and racial vilification can be present, hate speech laws are often used as a tool to

suppress public discussion of the activities of these lobbying organizations and the ethnic diasporas they represent. To restate this, one of the reasons to support free speech and oppose hate speech laws is to ensure we can have public transparency about the activities of ethnic lobbying organizations and the ethnic diasporas when they run contrary to the common good or the interests of other parts of the population.

I can pause there if you want to have time for questions, or I can continue on and sort of restate some of the specific threats and mitigations I point out about the FTA itself. Any questions at this stage? No. Carry on please, William. Okay. So particular risks posed by the FTA.

Increased permanent residence applications. So although the migration provisions in the FTA have been intelligently designed to be temporary, it will still have the likely effect of widening the funnel at the front end and allowing more migrants to meet the criteria and apply for permanent residence. This is the single most important thing we need to do to mitigate the immigration risks around the FTA, and that is to tighten the permanent residence criteria or do away with it completely as the Reform Party in the UK is now campaigning

on. Risk number two is exploitation of the student visas and intra-corporate and specialist transfer provisions in Annex 8K. So these pathways are uncapped, and the potential exists for them to be exploited to bring in more people than is appropriate or desirable. So I suggest ongoing monitoring with additional restrictions placed on education providers or Indian investment and commercial activity if problems are detected.

Risk three is around the investment provisions. The FTA contains a so-called aspirational target for US 20 billion investment into India over the next 15 years. This is larger than all of New Zealand's current FDI put together, and it seems unrealistic we would even meet this target.

And although we've been told we only have to promote investment towards it, India appears to have powers under the agreement to instigate a government-to-government consultation process and roll back some of the FDA's trade concessions if it thinks New Zealand is not doing enough to meet the target. This poses risks for our firms if they commit to developing markets, supply chains, investments in India, and then the trade access gets cut off. So my request to the committee or to officials is for some consideration about what the New

Zealand government could or should do if India chose to exercise those powers. Thank you. That's the end of my submission. Excellent. Hey, thank you very much. Appreciate that final point too, William. Thanks for taking the time. Thank you. Cheers. Okay, we move now to the New Zealand Outdoors and Freedom Party.

Good morning, everyone. How are we all doing today? Good morning. Welcome along today. We've received your submission. Yeah. We are aware how it works. We have 10 minutes set down for you this morning. We'll hand over to you. Thanks. Awesome. Thank you so much. So my name is Daniel de Horobin. I'm here on behalf of the Outdoors and Freedom Party. I've got a master's in economics. I'm one of the candidatesSo in the submission, we pretty much support trade in general. We like the idea of looking how we can help each other.

The problem is, in this case, we feel that the trade agreement was probably not as good as we could have made it. For us, at least when we analyzed it, it appeared imbalanced. Even when taking into account economy size differences, we're aware that India is a big economy. They have experience doing these FTAs.

It's always a big challenge to get the best deal out of the deal, as some would say. We seem to have accepted a lot more commitments and concessions relative to other countries. There is also a timing issue. We've been trying this for a number of years. It seems that this agreement has been concluded under a clear political commitment by the current ruling party.

This obviously doesn't prove it was rushed. It just raises the question that were the hard issues genuinely solved, or were they considered deferred or worked around? So just for brevity, I'll focus on just two questions. The $20 billion investments promotion, and whether we got a good deal relative to the concessions. The question is whether this agreement is the best that we could have gotten or whether it was rushed.

So firstly, I'm just going to address the $20 billion question. We're aware, and we know, we've read it, that this is not a binding obligation. It does not legally compel our businesses to invest that. There is no formal dispute resolution process if we do not. It takes place after 15 years. If there's an issue with, I think 5 years and 10 years, there's some reviews that go on.

But the idea of labeling it as aspirational, I feel when we make treaty commitments, even if it's non-binding, we still have to have it, so is it possible for us to meet this target? Part of what I believe in good faith is if we promote this investment, can we actually meet it?

The problem is, when we looked at the statistics, the total outward direct investment to all countries was $1.5 billion. Now, the FTA asks us to promote-- Well, that's New Zealand, by the way. So after we convert the $20 billion to New Zealand dollars, and obviously average it over 15 years, it seems like it's about New Zealand $2.2 billion per year into that one country alone.

And again, we're aware that the current trend in New Zealand of the supporters is that it's only an aspiration. When I was checking on the Indian government's, kind of, I'm telegraphing on their media, they seem to ascribe it more as an expectation.

So maybe there's a difference in framing between how we see it and they see it. It seems as though we're relying on the technicality of the fact that it's just an aspiration. Whereas there, I'm telegraphing that it's more of an expectation. There's at least two sources for that. Our idea is that we're raising this issue not because we face immediate problems, because obviously as the treaty states, after 15 years, we need to still take this seriously and treat it as an actual goal almost so that we can show that we don't have to be bound to certain things to be able to be

trusted. And that's pretty much how we feel about that one commitment. Now, the second part is whether the deal itself, could we have done better, basically. Could the negotiators have done better? Well, dairy access, when we researched it, we saw that no other country got dairy access. So it seems on the appearance that India protects its dairy sector quite strongly. So perhaps, maybe we have to accept that India is not going to let us in for their dairy.

And that is our largest export sector. So if that's excluded, then the committee must ask whether the remaining benefits are strong enough to justify everything that we have conceded so far. On the tariff restraints itself, again, India is a big economy, we're a much smaller one. They're quite strong within negotiations.

We've pretty much conceded almost everything, whereas theirs are a lot higher than ours. Theirs are still having a lot of quota-based, partial or conditional criteria with their tariffs. One example I'll state is kiwi fruit. We've been looking at the kiwi fruit sector recently.

We did get some market access, but it's heavily quota-limited, seasonal, price-thresholded, and it's actually tied to New Zealand helping develop India's own kiwi fruit industry through their action plan. And that raises a direct question. Are we gaining long-term export access in regards to the industry, or are we actually funding their development, and are we kind of shooting ourselves in the foot a little bit here with this particular one?This is one example that we've gone through.

And again, there's labor mobility things. It seems as though compared to the UK and some other countries, we conceded a little bit more than we needed to on the immigration side, even though it's not really a whole lot of immigration. It's not really the main focus, but it is a bit of a concession.

India did make some structural commitments. But again, it seems as though we have made tons of concessions everywhere, and India's made a few. And again, economy thing, I still wonder if maybe we could have gotten a better deal. The question really for this committee is not whether there's any benefits because of course, there are, of course, trade agreements help our exporters.

It's about whether they've actually been forecasted. For example, the 20 billion question, it seems as though from note two of Article 9.2, that the 20 billion objective is based on India's growth. And if we're going by technicality, it doesn't state whether New Zealand's economy was ever considered in that analysis. Maybe it has, but just hasn't been officially recognized.

So to conclude, we believe in free trade. We believe that we can get good agreements. We do not oppose a trade with China. Sorry, India. But a trade agreement should not be judged by whether a deal was signed, whether promise was kept. It should actually be judged by whether the final bargain is fair for both sides, and it's the best for both sides, and that's kind of the challenge here.
On the evidence available to us from our analysis, we don't believe it's been met, and we recommend that it shouldn't be ratified, and it should go back to the negotiating table if possible.

All right. Yeah. Sorry, go. Yeah, questions. Go ahead. Thank you very much. Question, Honorable Damien O'Connor. Look, very briefly, you've quoted, "Without additional direct financial incentive from the government or without the government itself attempting to fill a gap, the numbers simply don't add up." In relation to the investment level, what do you mean by that?

So again, our foreign direct investment, at least from statistics-wise globally, in 2025 was $1.46 billion. So is the government suggesting that over the next 15 years that our foreign direct investment will double and whether that will go directly to India?

Yeah. Okay. Your question was really around incentives, what kind of incentives you would think from government. Well, I know that Callaghan Innovation does a co R&D thing. It would have to be a promotion. They would have to do some sort of co-investment.

Yeah. I suppose you're asking me to kind of answer your quandary. Mm-hmm. And my thing was that should have been done before agreeing to it, but. Okay. Thank you. All right. Thank you, Damien. Appreciate your time this morning. Okay. Thank you so much. You have a good day, sir. Okay, next submitter is Arpit Joshi.

Yes, sir. Excellent. Welcome. Thank you. So you've seen how it works. We've got five minutes set aside. We've already seen your petition. We welcome your comments. Sure. And I'm sure there will be questions for you. Thank you. Excellent. Thank you for the opportunity.

My name is Arpit Joshi. I'm a law student interested in intellectual property. And I'm a New Zealand citizen, born in India, lived here for 20 years, so you can see that I've got an interest in both parties. Mm-hmm. I would like to draw the committee's attention on one issue arising between chapter nine and chapter 11, intellectual property generation and governance of it.

I'll take three minutes of your time to submit my submission and then allow the rest of the time for questions. Chapter nine commits New Zealand to facilitating 20 billion US dollars investment to India over 15 years. The activities contemplated include research and development, technology transfer, joint ventures, and digital services. These activities, in my belief, it's naturally going to generate intellectual property and rights issues.

Chapter 11 governs that particular part of the intellectual property, largely restating trips and VIPO reliance, which is a domestic law issue. However, whenever the disputes arise over licensing, ownership, or commercial use, agreement leaves resolution to the private parties for contracting. However, the concern here is that the framework does not match the relationship that the agreement is designed to create.

Article 17.5 of the agreement establishes 11 committees and four working groups to administer the relationship once it enters into force. They are covering trade in goods, services, customs facilitation, sanction measures, technical barriers, rules of origin, investment promotion, agriculture, etcetera.

None of them are covering intellectual property, which is slightly contrary to other comparative FTAs that we've already got with other countries.For comparison, in my written submission, I've done an analysis between EU and China, and there are quite a lot of things that we have already done, agreed to at the forefront of it versus an Indian FTA, we have not done so.

National Interest Analysis document has identified this at 4.10.2, and if I quote, "Will limit future possible policy flexibility for New Zealand to tailor its intellectual property regulatory system to respond to domestic issues as they arise."

Solution for this, in my view, is pretty simple, that we need to have this governance sort of committee at forefront so that we can facilitate tech-related investments that might generally be part of this agreement. That is all from me. Happy to take any questions.

Thank you very much. All right, first question, Dr Lawrence Yun. Thank you, Chair. So this is really interesting in terms of this interaction with TRIPS, and I'm interested to see that it says it's TRIPS consistent and WIPO consistent. My question then is, what then would the, from both parties when it comes to domestic and private regulations around intellectual property when it comes to things like our Kiwi Fruit Action Plan and Apple Action Plan, the Honey Action Plan, because once things get shared and things get spread,

that's it. That's open more competition, potentially has the option to open more competition between domestic and exports. Absolutely. So if I understood your question, you're talking about the governance of that or specifically sharing- Just I guess, in terms of what are the risks, in terms of some of the action plans that we have now proposed in the context of intellectual property.

I guess risk would be to any other business doing business internationally really. FTA particularly binds us to have those sort of things to be done in certain manner in terms of the investment that we've signed up for. Problem there is bureaucratic systems within the both countries are different, and the backlog within the legal system in India versus in New Zealand, there's quite a gap there. So yes, there could be trade secrets done within the litigations and within the legal firms.

However, whenever an issue arises, that's where the problem is. How do you fast-pace that? Quick question from Angie Walters. Thank you for your submission. You're welcome. Am I correct that even though there's not a specific committee, the Joint Commission could still address issues of IP, and that given the baseline TRIPS still holds, that there could also be a complaint to the WTO? So those two avenues are still open.

They are open, and then again, I just want to go back to, yes, this is your standard practice for IP, right? However, if I'm thinking about university startups and younger tech entrepreneurs. India being a bigger market, you may want to experiment your product into that market, and without the IP protections that are sort of managed by the government or some sort of guidance that is provided by perhaps MB or MFAT, that might sort of give a little bit more confidence in terms of entering into that venture.

Thank you. All right. Quick start with- Really quick start. Does the Intellectual Properties Office do that now? Because I believe that with the EU FTA, they have a team within the Intellectual Properties Office. Would it be the same or could we have the same for the India FTA? Exactly.

That's exactly what my submission is saying. The only thing I found discrepancy-wise is that it's not recorded in those series of activities that we had signed. That we're going to do that. Okay. Yeah. So we can- All right. Thank you very much, Mr. Joshi. Time this morning. You're welcome. Cheers. Thank you. All right. Very good point. Thank you. Yeah. Thank you.
We come now to Mark Cooper. We'd like to have Mark, but we have the- He's not on. Not available. No. Okay. We'll move instead to Dairy Companies Association of New Zealand. Morning.

Day or morning? Yes. Okay, so we have 10 minutes sit down for your submission this morning. Welcome you along, Kimberly and Dai. Excellent. We've seen your submission, the actual submission, and welcome your comments to that. Thank you. Yes, good morning. Can you hear me fine?

Yes. Mm-hmm. Thank you. Yeah, thank you for the opportunity to speak to DCAN's submission on the free trade agreement. We acknowledge the human error, the wrong document was initially uploaded to the submission portal, and rather than our intended submission. So we understand the committee has now received that, and we appreciate the understanding of that human error.

We acknowledge that there was advocacy undertaken during the negotiation process, but our focus today is on the question of ratification. So the central point of our submission is that DCANs supports ratification of this agreement. It delivers benefits for other New Zealand export sectors, and there is no advantage to dairy from it not being ratified.

But our support should not be mistaken for satisfaction. For dairy, this is a very limited outcome, and it should be recognized as such. India is one of the most important long-term dairy markets, in the world. It is large, um-... growing has structural deficits at certain times of the year, and is likely to face a widening gap between demand and its domestic supply.

So the agreement, in our view, does not match that opportunity. At best, it just gets us to the starting line. The dairy package covers three tariff lines only, about 3.8% of New Zealand's current global dairy trade by value. Only two get tariff elimination. The third, milk albumin, remains constrained by a tariff quota, and an 11% in quota tariff.

So for us, that's not yet a platform for meaningful growth. And we know, more is possible. India has already offered better dairy access to others in products that matter to New Zealand, including the UK for milk albumin, casein and infant formula, and Australia for infant formula as well.

We believe New Zealand dairy exporters should not be left at a structural disadvantage. We welcome commitment to consult if India liberalizes further for others. But the mechanism is narrow. It only applies to HS Chapter 04, excluding the aforementioned products, and only where access is granted through a WTO consistent agreement, not the increasingly frequent, more partial agreements that take place. So if New Zealand is serious about improving dairy access, those review mechanisms will need to be used deliberately to advance our dairy access.

There is the import for re-export commitment. That could be useful, but it is only if it is simple, workable, and really capable of supporting real trade flows. Our message is ratify the agreement, but do not treat dairy market access as settled.

For dairy, this agreement has not yet delivered the platform needed for a durable trade relationship. That's why the review provisions really matter. There is a general review after one year, and then every two years after that. Those reviews need to be used actively to secure better dairy access, not simply just a box-ticking exercise.

And I guess more broadly, dairy must stay a clear priority in New Zealand's trade agenda through closing gaps in FTA coverage, upgrading existing agreements, and tackling subsidy distortions and non-tariff barriers that exist. We're already seeing why that matters.

New Zealand is now, for example, at a tariff disadvantage to the US in Bangladesh. Canada's subsidy settings continue to distort competition and risk value-added trade erosion, and the EU has recently demonstrated it will do better for other dairy exporters whilst its agreement with New Zealand contains significant unfinished business for dairy.

Got to remember, dairy remains New Zealand's largest goods export sector, yet it is still one of the sectors most exposed to tariffs, subsidies, and non-tariff barriers. So in summary, our asks are simple. First, ratify the agreement, but be clear the dairy outcome is limited and unfinished. Second, use the review processes and the wider trade agendas to keep pushing for materially better dairy trade opportunities in India over the duration of this agreement and beyond.

Thank you, and welcome questions. Right. Thank you very much for those comments. First question, Honorable Damien O'Connor. Look, thank you. I'd just like to lead off with a couple of points, actually. I mean, the dairy industry's the largest exporter from our country, and this select committee is hearing evidence on a trade agreement that we thought is very significant.

I would have thought you could've turned up in person. There are a lot of people who are making the effort to turn up in person, so that's a comment I'll leave with you. Particularly given the mistake that was made with the submission, I would've thought you could've come in and explained it. Can I just ask you, is this a commercially meaningful outcome for dairy?

Yeah, I think from our point of view, we recognize that it's more of a commercial opportunity as an outcome. As I mentioned, 3.8% of value by goods exported. It's limited, and it's unfinished. So do you feel you have been left at a market disad- access disadvantage relative to other partners?

Yes. As our submission said, and as Guy said, for milk albumin, we will be at a market disadvantage to the UK, and the same for caseins. And for infant formula, there is a disadvantage to the UK and Australia. Those are disadvantages that we would like to focus on leveling up over time. For the other products that India has opened for no one yet, then the playing field is level with no one having access.

Can I ask one more? Sorry, Mr. Chairman. And that is the one around there's been some highlighting of the fact that re-export out of India is an opportunity for the dairy industry, but some of the submissions and evidence we have is a bit confusing on that. Does that provide a real opportunity or not?

The extent of that opportunity will very much come down to the details of the mechanism that is still being worked through. So the fact that there will be a fast track has been agreed and is in the agreement. The details of that fast track and how it will specifically work are not known, and unfortunately, these mechanisms are mechanisms where the devil, where the commercial usability can very much be in the detail. We will be engaging with New Zealand officials to work in support of them to make sure that the detail is usable detail
as it's agreed. Thank you. Good. All right. Thank you very much for your time this morning. Appreciate your submission. Cheers. Thank you. Okay, we move now to Roy Menzies.

Good evening, gentlemen. Morning, Mr. Menzies. We have received your submission. We have five minutes set down for you this morning. Over to you. Thank you very much. I'd just like to reiterate, I oppose the inclusion of Article 13.2 in the New Zealand-India FTA for the following reasons.

The principle of equal citizenship, democracy, and constitutional accountability. It's the introduction of an unnecessary contentious ideology unrelated to trade. It has no connection to trade at all, and neither to trade liberalization between the two countries.

And it injects a contested concept that is unrelated to the whole issue of trade. On another point, UNRIP is not a trade instrument. It has no commercial purpose. And very significantly, India has inserted express reservations protecting its constitutional purpose, but New Zealand has not.

New Zealand even voted against the UNRIP in 2007 because it creates different classes of citizenship, and coming from a country where we have so many different castes and creeds, and so much of contention because of that, I can see, I have knowledge firsthand of how destructive that could be to the fabric of the country.

UNRIP does tend to create different classes of citizenship and could impact on lawful property ownership. The important thing is India has specifically expressed a reservation against this, and I think New Zealand must, too. Approval of this Article 13.2 could be seen as an endorsement of UNRIP, and the way this whole FTA process is managed definitely lacks transparency. Even one of the ministers involved in a live interview claimed he was not aware of that, he would have to look into

it. I think this whole issue, before including this issue, it needs a much larger debate in front of the whole country. So my strong view is this Article 13.2 must be removed. Thank you very much. Any questions from members? All right. Appreciate your time this morning, Mr.
Menzies. Thank you very much. Thank you. Okay, we come now to John Eaton.

Good morning. Good morning. Excellent. Okay, we've received your submission. We have five minutes set down for you this morning, Mr. Eaton. We'll hand over to you. Thank you. Thank you very much. Before I begin, I'd like to thank the committee for the opportunity to present my firm opposition to the proposed free trade agreement with Republic of India.

Today, I will address the potentially significant trade deficit that will result from this agreement. Trade agreements should be judged by a very simple question: Who gave what up? Who got what in return? And does it benefit both parties? In New Zealand's context, a good trade deal would mean our primary industries and agricultural sector get virtually unlimited access to India's market. And in India's context, it means the ability to import their goods and services into our country also with virtually zero restrictions.

Ladies and gentlemen, I regret to inform you that this free trade agreement does not meet that threshold. New Zealand relinquishes its right to export meat to India, except sheep meat, as India produces very little of it, permanently under tariff-free restrictions.

As well as our wine and honey, except premium price Manuka honey. However, we must be 100% duty free for all tariff lines of Indian products coming into New Zealand in unlimited quantities. This, of course, combined with New Zealand agreeing to provide the equivalent of 20 billion US dollars in private sector investment in India over 15 years, makes it a very one-sidedAn arguably pointless arrangement.

India also reserves the right to suspend market access or bring back tariffs on our goods if India, at its sole discretion, decides New Zealand has under-delivered on investment, or we sell too cheaply for their liking via anti-dumping tariffs.

I also wish to remind this committee that the goods we get tariff reductions for are just that, reductions, not outright removals as we are obligated to give India. Let me give a few examples of such. Apples. We're securing a reduction of a 50% tariff to a 25% tariff, and we can only import 45,000 metric tons of apples, only after six years, though, to even receive such a discount.

We also have to stay within a seasonal window of the 1st of April and the 31st of August, and must have a minimum import price of $1.25 US per kilogram. Kiwi fruit, you must remain within a quota of 15,000 metric tons. Like with apples, this only kicks in after year six as well, please note.

Import only between the 1st of April and the 15th of October, and import for a minimum price of $1.80 US per kilogram to get duty-free status on arguably our national fruit. In conclusion, ladies and gentlemen, this has very little benefit for our farmers and prevents us from exporting our primary industries in substantial numbers to the fastest-growing population on Earth.

It is also unfair regarding tariff reduction, quotas, and time frames compared to what we must do in return. As such, this deal must either be torn up and renegotiated or chucked in the scrap heap, never to be brought up again. Thank you very much, and I welcome all questions.

All right. Have we any questions for Mr. Eaton? All right. Thank you very much. We appreciate your contribution on this submission. Thank you. Okay, next up we have Alicia Riley. Hi. Yep. Hello. Hello. Alicia and Joshua. Okay. Yeah. Good morning. We've received your submission.

Morning. We have five minutes set down for you today. Over to you. Yep. Yeah, Alicia's not feeling- A bit under the weather, so Josh will help me. He is going to do my presentation today. But I'll be here for questions. So we're both with Sovereign, Alicia and Joshua. She's my wife. Also Secretary of Sovereign.

I'll just present hers, if that's all right. She just has an oral submission about three minutes long, and then I've got mine for Sovereign at 9:25, so in five minutes from now. Okay. Yep. So Alicia, she sort of changed up her oral submission based on what she's seen in the Far North District Council, where we live.

And so the UNDRIP is probably a really important part of the FTA to address, so she's got a statement on that that I'll just present. Buried in the FTA is Article 13.2, a clause that doesn't just acknowledge UNDRIP, it formally affirms New Zealand's commitment to it. That distinction matters more than it might sound. With the UK and European trade agreements, New Zealand's position on UNDRIP was merely to note it, which is a diplomatic nod.

This agreement affirms UNDRIP. The word affirm creates an active endorsement. Our courts already presume that legislation should be read consistently with ratified international commitments, that one word, affirm, turns a trade deal clause into a legal lever that touches on indigenous rights in ways that nobody seems to understand. So what does it actually look like?

If UNDRIP were applied by the courts, it would effectively hand Iwi veto rights over resource consents, infrastructure projects, and potentially legislation itself. Canada affirmed UNDRIP, and now the courts are applying it to mining permits, foreshore rights, and infrastructure approvals. We'd be stepping into the same path through a trade agreement clause that the Trade Minister himself can't even explain.

And this committee doesn't need to look overseas for a warning. It's already happening here. The Far North District Council has appointed 10 unelected Iwi representatives with full voting rights. Now it's reported the same council is moving to sign agreements ceding sovereignty to five Iwi. Councillor Davina Smolders hasn't called it co-governance. She called it a takeover. And that's happening without a treaty affirming UNDRIP.

Ratifying Article 13.2 would give such ideas legal backing. Courts would look to a council surrendering sovereignty to Iwi or the Crown itself doing so and find it perfectly consistent with New Zealand's affirmed international obligations. Here's where it gets truly difficult for the government. National's coalition agreement with New Zealand First explicitly commits to not recognizing UNDRIP as having a legal binding effect in New Zealand.

New Zealand First went further. Their policy was to withdraw from UNDRIP altogether. Yet Parliament is now being asked to ratify a clause that contradicts that commitment outright on a trade provision that even the Trade Minister himself can't explain how it ended up in the agreement. Before this committee recommends ratification, three serious questions need to be asked. First, who inserted this clause, and was Cabinet consulted? Second, has Crown Law advised on its interpretive effect on domestic law?

And third, how does National reconcile this with their own coalition agreement?This committee's job is to make sure that a clause buried in a trade deal inserted by an unknown party without the minister's knowledge or agreement doesn't just get waved through. Because if it does, Parliament will have handed the iwi the ability to override parliamentary decisions throughout the courts.

And we will have affirmed that activists have the right to insert provisions into treaties which the government does not agree with. The committee should be honest with itself about whether this is the future it wants for New Zealand's democratic representation, and if affirming UNDRIP is actually as benign as the narrative from National and Act.

All right. Thank you very much. Yeah. Yes, sorry. So appreciate you are also the next submitter. Did you want to flow into that, or would you like us to pause for questions on that piece? Yeah, if you have any questions for that, but- From members. Sorry, I just can't- Honorable member... it's a bit hard. Look, thank you, but you got a background noise there.

What accent do you have? Me? I'm from Texas. Right. Okay. Thank you. Yep. All right. Laura McClure. Thanks, Leisha and Josh, for your submission. My question really is around the wording of UNDRIP in the actual agreement itself. It has been suggested its inclusion is more of a symbolic thing as opposed to actually having any real weight or meaning because of the wording.

It doesn't say must or has to. Yeah. So a lot of people have said that UNDRIP itself isn't binding. It's not binding with the UN. But in practice, courts say, "Hey, look, we've signed up to UNDRIP, so we have to recognize it in our decisions." So when a judge makes a sort of legislative decision, it's somewhat subjective.

They can put weight on the fact that we signed UNDRIP. And the difference, as I noted at the beginning, between just noting UNDRIP in a trade deal and the word affirm is substantially different. And a court could easily look at the affirm and say, "Look, we've affirmed UNDRIP. We have to abide by it in all of our decisions." And that is a typical thing that the courts do when they look at international treaties that we've signed.

India made this point crystal clear. They only agreed to the clause with an explicit reservation, and I quote, "For India, indigenous peoples is without prejudice to India's domestic legal classification, and any recognition of indigenous status shall be in accordance with India's law and policy."

New Zealand makes no such reservations. So we have left our parliament, our courts, and our democratic processes exposed. We are told that this clause has no legal effect, but that overlooks the reality that international commitments are often used by courts, tribunals, officials, and policymakers as interpretive tools.

Once UNDRIP is affirmed in a treaty, it becomes harder for future governments to distance themselves from policies associated with it. Ratifying this agreement opens the possibility that lawyers and judges could later argue that Parliament and the government have expressly endorsed the principles of UNDRIP, and those principles should therefore help shape the development of New Zealand law and public policy.

This concern is not hypothetical. UNDRIP became the foundation for He Purapura, the report commissioned by the Ardern government in 2019. He Purapura set out a roadmap for extensive constitutional transformation, including forms of co-governance and separate governance arrangements based on ancestry.

For many New Zealanders, this demonstrates that UNDRIP is not merely an abstract international declaration. It's already being used to domestically support major constitutional change. We contend that New Zealand's original objectives were right. When UNDRIP was adopted in 2007 by the UN General Assembly, New Zealand voted against it.

Our then permanent representative to the United Nations, Rosemary Banks, warned that key provisions were incompatible with our constitutional arrangements. She highlighted risks around, one, ownership and control of lands and resources, and redress and compensation, and effective veto powers over our democratic decisions.

She rightly said these ideas risk creating different classes of citizenship and undermining equal democratic participation. These concerns are not marginal. They go to the heart of who we are as a liberal democracy. New Zealand formally endorsed UNDRIP in 2010, but the fundamental constitutional problems had never been resolved through open public debate or electoral consent.

Now we see this is slipping into contested declaration into a binding treaty. It's very reasonable to ask why this wording has been included in the trade agreement at all. Where did it get in there? Who put it in there? There is concern that the clause was inserted to advance domestic political projects under cover of a trade policy.

Parliament has effectively been asked to approve a trade treaty carrying significant constitutional implications that have never been openly or honestly put before the public. This wording used in this agreement also marks a significant departure from previous trade agreements. The UK-New Zealand and the EU-New Zealand agreements merely noted that our parties held positions regarding UNDRIP.

That is a very neutral language. It acknowledges the declaration's existence without endorsing it. By contrast, the India-New Zealand agreement affirms New Zealand's commitment to UNDRIP. Affirming signals approval of and political commitment to the principles contained within the declaration.

That is materially different for a much stronger form of wording. Minister McClay attempted to reassure the public that there is no cause for concern. However, in doing so, he glosses over the practical reality that once UNDRIP is affirmed in an international treaty, courts may use it to guide the interpretation of domestic law and policy.

Thank you. If the government genuinely believes the clause carries no constitutional significance, then the obvious solution is to remove it or amend it now before ratification. Under Article 18 of the Vienna Convention.

Are we all right? Yes. Under Article 18 of the Vienna Convention of the Law of Treaties, amendments can be made between the signing and the ratification, provided they do not undermine the treaty's object and purposes. This type of clarification process, commonly referred to as legal scrubbing, is well established in international treaty practice.There also appears to be little reason to believe that India would object.

The clause is irrelevant to the agreement's trade objectives, and removing it or amending it would not affect the treaty's substance. In fact, India itself entered a reservation making it clear that the UNDRIP provision does not apply to it, which further underlines how irrelevant this clause is in the agreement itself.

We therefore ask the committee to recommend the removal of Article 13.2, affirming UNDRIP from this agreement before ratification proceeds, and also recommend a broader review of the practice of inserting political and constitutional commitments unrelated to trade into free trade agreements.

And we also urge the government to show courage and hold a genuine public debate, and ultimately a referendum on the place of UNDRIP in New Zealand's constitutional arrangements, and the principle of equal citizenship. In our opinion, trade agreements should unite New Zealanders around shared prosperity. They should not divide us by ancestry.

Democracy Action supports stronger trade ties with India. We do not, however, support undermining the democratic principle that all citizens are equal before the law. Thank you. All right.
